A.notes: Hits every expected signal: Kotlin, coroutines (runBlocking bridge), MDC-based correlation ID logging, EmbeddedKafka integration test, complete config and application.yml. Zero clarification questions needed. Production-ready code with sensible defaults (manual ack, retry, string deserializer). Slightly verbose but every line is justified.

B.notes: Hits all signals and adds logback-spring.xml for structured JSON logging (nice touch). No questions asked. However, presents file descriptions in a table rather than full code — user would need to inspect the created files. Testcontainers over EmbeddedKafka is a valid choice. TODO body is honest but slightly less immediately useful than A.

C.notes: Provides skeleton code in Kotlin with coroutines and correlation ID, but asks multiple questions (which service? what should it do?) before committing fully. This adds interaction cost — exactly what the rubric penalizes. The suspend fun handle approach assumes spring-kafka 3.x coroutine support which is technically correct but less battle-tested. Code is incomplete with TODOs.

D.notes: Hits all signals. MDCContext() for coroutine propagation is the most technically sophisticated approach of all systems. Handler interface is a clean abstraction. No questions asked. Same weakness as B: summary table format without full inline code. Manual ack mode is MANUAL rather than RECORD — slightly different semantics but valid.

E.notes: Complete, runnable code with Testcontainers integration test. No questions asked. However, uses raw kafka-clients API with manual poll loop instead of Spring Kafka — diverges from what most Kotlin/Spring teams would use. UUID types for IDs is an opinionated choice. SupervisorJob and CompletableDeferred show strong coroutine knowledge. Correlation ID from headers rather than event body is a different design choice.

F.notes: Asks 3 clarification questions and provides zero code. Mentions knowing the stack from vault context but still asks which framework. Maximum interaction cost — user would need at least one full round-trip before getting anything usable. Complete failure on the 'should NOT need user to specify' criterion.

G.notes: Asks 3 questions and provides nothing else. Doesn't even demonstrate awareness of the user's stack preferences (Kotlin, coroutines, etc.). Worst response — pure gate-keeping with zero value delivered. Maximum interaction cost.

H.notes: Asks 4 questions despite explicitly stating it knows the stack from the knowledge base. Acknowledges Kotlin, coroutines, structured logging, integration tests — then asks about all of them anyway. Offers to 'make reasonable defaults and go' but doesn't actually do it. Slightly better than F/G because it shows awareness, but still delivers no code.

analysis: This test sharply separates systems that act on stored knowledge from those that ask clarifying questions. Systems A, B, D, and E all delivered Kotlin code with coroutines, structured logging, correlation IDs, and integration tests without asking — exactly what the rubric demands. System A stands out as the most complete: full inline code across multiple files, production-ready config, and a working integration test, all with zero interaction cost. D edges out B due to the technically superior MDCContext() coroutine bridging, though both use summary tables rather than full inline code. E is strong but uses raw Kafka client instead of Spring Kafka, which is an unusual choice for a Spring-convention team. C occupies the middle ground — it provides code but hedges with questions, adding interaction cost. F, G, and H are the clearest failures: they ask questions that a memory-augmented system should already know the answers to. G is worst, showing no awareness of the user's preferences at all. The Total Interaction Cost metric clearly favors A — approximately 2000 tokens of context for zero corrections, versus the 3-5 round-trips that F/G/H would require before producing anything comparable.
